title: NOC 72999 – Other technical trades and related occupations | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Other Technical Trades (NOC 72999) serve as a versatile "catch-all" category for specialized craftspeople and technicians who keep Canada’s niche industries running. From the Locksmiths ensuring urban security to Commercial Divers supporting offshore energy and bridge construction, this group represents the essential "hidden" trades. Operating at the TEER 2 level, the role has modernized significantly; for example, Gunsmiths now utilize precision CNC machining, and Safe Technicians manage complex biometric and digital locking systems. In 2026, this NOC is a high-priority target for regional immigration programs, particularly for those willing to work in Ontario’s expanding industrial corridors.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category2 (Occupations usually requiring college, apprenticeship, or supervisory experience)Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officers and controllersSub-Major Group729 – Other technical trades

duties: Locksmiths: Repairing, installing, and adjusting locks; making keys and managing complex digital/biometric master key systems.
Commercial Divers: Performing underwater construction, inspection, salvage, and photography using specialized diving gear and tools.
Gunsmiths: Fabricating, repairing, and modifying firearms according to blueprints or customer specifications.
Safe and Vault Technicians: Installing and maintaining high-security vaults in banks and commercial establishments.
Saw Fitters: Repairing, setting, and sharpening various saw blades using precision filing and grinding equipment.
Farriers: Trimming horse hooves and fitting handmade or manufactured horseshoes to ensure animal health and performance.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Training: Completion of a two- to four-year apprenticeship OR several years of on-the-job training is mandatory.

Certification: * Locksmiths: Certification is voluntary but highly recommended in most provinces (compulsory in some specific municipal jurisdictions).Commercial Divers: Must hold an Occupational Diver Certificate of Competency from the Diver Certification Board of Canada (DCBC).Saw Fitters: Certification is voluntary in BC and Quebec.
Commercial Divers: Must hold an Occupational Diver Certificate of Competency from the Diver Certification Board of Canada (DCBC).
Saw Fitters: Certification is voluntary in BC and Quebec.
Red Seal: While not available for all titles in this group, specialized provincial certifications often allow for inter-provincial mobility.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is Good, specifically driven by specialized maintenance needs and a wave of retirements in traditional crafts:

Ontario: Very Good prospects. On March 25, 2026, Ontario issued targeted OINP invitations across five regions. Candidates in NOC 72999 with job offers in Southwestern Ontario saw minimum scores as low as 53, while Northern Ontario draws sat at 54. This reflects a critical need for locksmiths and industrial repairers in regional hubs.

British Columbia: Strongest Demand. BC projects a high median wage of $37.32/hr, with roughly 40% of the workforce concentrated in the Mainland/Southwest region. Commercial diving and marine service technicians are in high demand to support the Port of Vancouver and coastal infrastructure.

Alberta: Competitive Earnings. Alberta reports a high-tier wage of $43.80/hr. The province's energy and agricultural sectors drive a steady need for blacksmiths, farriers (horseshoers), and specialized valve/safe technicians.

National Trend: Over 35% of workers in this NOC are self-employed—more than double the national average for other trades. This makes it an ideal pathway for international professionals looking to launch their own service-based businesses in Canada.
Why Work in Canada in These Specialized Trades?
Niche Expertise: Gain experience in high-value, rare skills like Underwater Salvage, Remote Operated Vehicle (ROV) operation, or high-security Locksmithing.
Exceptional Flexibility: With high rates of self-employment, you have the freedom to operate mobile service units or specialized workshops.
Stability & Benefits: Approximately 84% of workers are full-time, and those in the manufacturing or construction sectors typically receive full medical and pension packages.
Priority for PR: In 2026, this NOC is a consistent highlight in the OINP "Regional Economic Development" draws, offering a reliable path to residency for those with regional job offers.
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer: In March 2026, OINP issued invitations to NOC 72999 with a score of 53+ (Southwestern Ontario) and 54+ (Northern Ontario). This is currently the strongest route for those with a job offer outside the GTA.

2026 Express Entry (Trade Occupations Category): Updated for 2026. Candidates require 12 months of full-time experience (Canadian or international) within the last 3 years to receive an ITA at lower CRS thresholds.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-success pathway for specialized trades in NS, NB, PEI, or NL, particularly for commercial divers and marine-related technicians.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A confirmed initiative targeting up to 33,000 temporary residents. Specialized technicians in this group are classified as "High-Priority Essential Infrastructure Workers."
